title: What about toxin doses for older patients？ There may be instances where adjusting the dose is necess
filename: What about toxin doses for older patients？ There may be instances where adjusting the dose is necess.en.vtt
excerpt: But do you need to make changes to dosages when injecting older patients in many areas of the face it's a very similar strength muscle with different levels of supporting tissue and this doesn't always make a difference I don't think it makes a great big difference when you're tr…

segments.text: but do you need to make changes to dosages when injecting older patients in many areas of the face it's a very similar strength muscle with different levels of supporting tissue and this doesn't always make a difference I don't think it makes a great big difference when you're treating the LaBella on a 68 year old as opposed to 25 year olds similar dosages if you want a little bit of movement or no movement you're still going to have to treat in a similar way now that's very different with the frontalis and potentially with the orbicularis oculi because there's less support for the tissues in other ways probably the best example is orbicularisoculi because when you're treating crow's feet in an older patient and you just relax the muscle it's very easy to relax the muscle and improve the lines the problem is that as you get older the degree of support relative to the other structures that the muscle gives the cheek is higher essentially the orbicular circular is supporting the cheek and so if you use the same dose in a younger person it makes them look older they actually sag so you get the sad look as the cheek descends because
the orbicularis oculi is no longer supporting the cheek in its resting position

keyPoints.text: But do you need to make changes to dosages when injecting older patients in many areas of the face it's a very similar strength muscle with different levels of supporting tissue and this doesn't always make a difference …

transcript: but do you need to make changes to dosages when injecting older patients in many areas of the face it's a very similar strength muscle with different levels of supporting tissue and this doesn't always make a difference I don't think it makes a great big difference when you're treating the LaBella on a 68 year old as opposed to 25 year olds similar dosages if you want a little bit of movement or no movement you're still going to have to treat in a similar way now that's very different with the frontalis and potentially with the orbicularis oculi because there's less support for the tissues in other ways probably the best example is orbicularisoculi because when you're treating crow's feet in an older patient and you just relax the muscle it's very easy to relax the muscle and improve the lines the problem is that as you get older the degree of support relative to the other structures that the muscle gives the cheek is higher essentially the orbicular circular is supporting the cheek and so if you use the same dose in a younger person it makes them look older they actually sag so you get the sad look as the cheek descends because the orbicularis oculi is no longer supporting the cheek in its resting position
